posts.caption:
In 1977, in Minneapolis, a 19-year-old musician named Prince Rogers Nelson caught the attention of music agent Owen Husney and his partner Gary Levinson. Keen to have him photographed, they approached @robertwhitmanphoto, playing an early demo of Soft and Wet to draw him in. It worked. Whitman was transfixed, and soon after photographed Nelson, who would become the artist known as Prince.⁠
⁠
Ten years after his passing, we revisit those images collected in Prince Pre Fame, a book that gathers the full series. As Whitman puts it, it’s “a book that every Prince fan should have on their table, telling the story of two guys from Minneapolis following their passions, trying to pursue their dreams.”⁠
⁠
📸 Prince Pre Fame. Photography @robertwhitmanphoto
posts.alt_text: Photo by AnOther Magazine on April 21, 2026. May be a black-and-white image of child, hair, poster, magazine and text that says 'AnOther ther: Candid and Unseen Photos of a Pre- Pre-Fame Fame Prince'.

posts.caption:
The Dutch photographers @inezandvinoodh met in the early 1980s, as students at the Fashion Academy Vogue in Amsterdam. Their first collaboration took place in 1986, when Matadin commissioned Van Lamsweerde to photograph a fashion show for his short-lived clothing line, Lawina. Six years later, the duo fell in love, their romantic relationship taking shape alongside their already-burgeoning artistic partnership. And they haven’t looked back. ⁠
⁠
Four decades on, Inez and Vinoodh are considered two of the most important image-makers of our time. Titled Can Love Be a Photograph?, a new exhibition at @kunstmuseum.nl takes place across 16 rooms and is presented thematically, under titles like ‘(Un)Real’ and ‘The Psychomorphic Phenomenon’, in a way that corresponds to the intimate or expansive nature of each space.⁠
⁠
“We questioned truth and time in photography from day one. We were always excited about being able to disrupt time by going in after the shoot, on the computer, and toying with this idea of reality versus surrealism,” the pair tells @daisyaecw. Read the interview at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Can Love Be A Photograph? Courtesy of the artists and @kunstmuseum.nl⁠
1. Inez & Vinoodh, Kate, 1999⁠
2. Inez & Vinoodh, Think Love, 2025⁠
4. Inez & Vinoodh, Prince, 2013⁠
5. Inez & Vinoodh, Fashion Plate no. 3, 2020⁠
7. Inez & Vinoodh, A$AP Rocky, RZA & Rihanna – Vogue UK, 2023⁠
⁠
#Inez&Vinoodh

posts.caption:
Lois Weber, Hypocrites (1915) “a morality tale that finds parallels between the medieval period and 1910s America. Weber takes aim at the clergy, politicians, and the ruling class over 100 years ago. Margaret Edwards plays Truth, and she appears nude throughout a dream sequence, with Weber photographing her in double exposure, giving Truth a ghostly appearance.”
*
Germaine Dulac, The Smiling Madame Beudet, (1922) A complex, expressionist film about a troubled marriage includes daydreams that represent the protagonist’s inner world, in which she drives away in her own motorcar, or unsettling visions where her husband crawls in through a window.
*
Elvira Notari, a pioneer of Italian silent cinema (1875–1946), was renowned for her realistic, gritty Neapolitan melodramas, many of which featured poetic, subjective sequences that operated as dreams, memories, or internal fantasies rather than straight narrative.
*
✨Women were incredibly influential in the silent film era as directors, writers, producers, and editors ✨ let’s remember to give them their flowers too.

posts.caption:
One of the most visionary artists in the world, David Bowie continues to inspire across generations. David Bowie: You’re Not Alone, an exhibition where his music, art, and multi-layered universe can be experienced in an immersive 360-degree environment, is now showing at @_lightroomldn in London. It features a carefully curated selection from thousands of items housed in the Bowie archive, including performance footage, rare interviews, and even his paintings. Set within an 11-meter-high space, the exhibition offers an overwhelmingly immersive experience.
While his reputation was already well known, for the editor who did not live through his era, this exhibition became a moment to truly realise just how innovative and artistic Bowie was. At the same time, it was an opportunity to appreciate the enduring influence he continues to have today. This immersive exhibition runs until October, so I highly recommend checking the details below and considering a visit.
📌 “David Bowie: You’re Not Alone” Details
• Date: 22 April - 10 October
• Location: @_lightroomldn, 12 Lewis Cubitt Square
• Fee: Adults (£25), Students & Under 18s (£15)
Special thanks to @outsideorg
Words by Julie Jeong
Next-Level Fashion, Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
📷 @a2z_fashionmag
#DavidBowie #LightRoom

posts.caption:
Massimo Dutti presented its SS26 Limited Edition collection through a 10-day pop-up in Paris, introducing the collection through an installation built around art, publishing, and gastronomy rather than a runway or traditional retail space.
Vintage furniture, artworks, and curated books were placed throughout, shifting the environment closer to an exhibition than a store, with collaborators including Yvon Lambert Bookshop, chef Rose Chalalai, and coffee by Café Nuances contributing to the overall experience.
The collection focused on restrained construction using linen, silk, suede, and leather, with an emphasis on proportion, drape, and precision tailoring, while neutral tones carried throughout, aligning the garments with the space and positioning the project as a broader cultural environment rather than a standalone retail moment.

posts.caption:
Between walking runways and fronting campaigns internationally to her silver screen debut in ‘Anyone but You’, @charleefraser is a multi-talented woman. But among Fraser’s manifold skills is one talent that she has, until now, kept on the quiet. “I love roller skating,” she shares. “I’m constantly thinking with the work that I do and [my] schedule. I’m a major organiser – such a Capricorn,” she laughs. “[With] my skates on, the only thing I can think about is what I’m doing. And it just really grounds me, brings me back into that present.”
Through the link in bio, see Charlee Fraser photographed at sunrise on the Northern Beaches of Sydney for BAZAAR.
Charlee Fraser wears @ralphlauren Collection in the April 2026 issue of Harper’s BAZAAR Australia/New Zealand.

posts.caption:
“Oh, man, little baby Charlee,” @charleefraser chuckles as she reflects on her career beginnings. “It was really interesting because I wasn’t looking to model at the time – the opportunity kind of presented itself,” she says of her beginning. By 2016, Fraser had been modelling locally for three years, with early gigs including runway appearances for David Jones, Carla Zampatti and Bec + Bridge. “My agency was like, ‘I think it’s time for you to go overseas. You’ve done everything here. Go spread the wings’,” she says. “At the time, I really felt ready for that.”
Continue reading Charlee Fraser’s interview with BAZAAR at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
For #BazaarIndia’s 17th anniversary issue, actor Ananya Panday (@AnanyaPanday) shares that she loves being alone now. “I used to be someone who couldn’t be alone at all. I’d be scared to sit alone for even 10 minutes. I always needed someone around me — my partner, my parents, my friends,” admits Panday. “But when I started living alone, even though I’m in the same building as my parents, that little bit of space changed everything. Building your own environment and doing things your own way has shifted my love for space.”
Much like our cover installation, which conveys the negotiation of the fragile space between two people, the artwork by How Are You Feeling Studio features two film cranes facing each other like figures. Their machine bodies, covered in soft fabric, suggest an unexpected intimacy.
Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Harper’s Bazaar India’s March cover star Ananya Panday (@AnanyaPanday) is unafraid of being vulnerable. She’s all too aware of the impermanence of fame and of not taking things for granted. “I definitely still have some sense of imposter syndrome. If I hear my name out loud, like someone saying ‘Ananya Panday’ or introducing me somewhere, for a second I kind of disassociate and I can’t fully respond to it,” she admits.
Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

posts.caption:

The glamour exists, but it’s only one part of a job that demands far more than just “good taste.” It’s fast-paced, detail-heavy, and always on. posts.caption:
Nowadays, it’s not just the party invitation that matters, but the photograph you get when you’re there. Not only are brands investing in more stylistic content, but celebrities, influencers and wealthy shoppers are hiring their own photographers to ensure a steady stream of perfectly staged pictures.
In a fast changing media landscape, where AI is starting to encroach on brand work, working directly with party and event goers is a growing revenue stream for photographers.
BoF’s Joan Kennedy (@joooankennedyyy) explores how event photographers are evolving into on-demand image makers — and why it’s becoming a lucrative new lane. Read more #linkinbio
📷 @germanlarkin, @ryancsides, @byholliem

posts.caption:
On the eve of Milan Design Week, fifteen of the industry’s most influential founders, executives and creative directors gathered at Lake Como’s newly opened Edition hotel for an intimate, off-the-record conversation about where luxury goes next.
Co-hosted by Imran Amed, founder and editor-in-chief of The Business of Fashion and Tina Edmundson, president of luxury at Marriott International, the evening brought together senior executives, creative directors and founders from Burberry, Etro, Augustinus Bader, Jimmy Choo, Dimore Studio, Gianvito Rossi, La Double J, Giuliva Heritage and more.
“Luxury has had an extraordinary run, but we’re entering a more complex phase, where some of the definitions we’ve relied on around value, exclusivity and creativity are being tested,” shared Amed, in his welcoming remarks.
The intimate setting enabled candid, cross-industry dialogue — and fostered meaningful relationships at a time of structural change across the sector.
📷 Alberto Feltrin
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by The Business of Fashion on April 20, 2026 tagging @smaino, @edward_l_buchanan, @luxurygroup.official, and @editioncomo. May be an image of one or more people, suit, champagne, wine glass and text.

posts.caption: Honestly this post was pointless and teaches absolutely nothing. If BOF truly cared about independent designers, they could use their platform to actually champion them rather than solely focusing on luxury houses, I used to have a lot of respect for Imran Amed but in recent months every time I come across anything on BOF, that respect is completely gone. You have sold yourselves out to the highest bidder and take the glory and funding that luxury brands are willing to pay you while patronising a generation of independent designers. Of which most of them aren’t even able to go on to BOF because they need a paid subscription to do so. Enough with the gas lighting, please stop pretending anyone within BOF actually cares about independent designers’ struggles while you continue to write a bunch of nonsense.

posts.caption:
The global fashion industry is a $2.5 trillion economic engine, and yet in the corridors of Washington and high finance, it's often treated as a sideshow. This week BoF founder Imran Amed was in DC at Semafor World Economy, listening to conversations about AI and genomics and energy — and arguing that fashion is actually one of the best barometers we have for where the global consumer is heading.
This week on The BoF Podcast, @ImranAmed sits down with @Zegna's Ermenegildo Zegna, @RalphLauren's Patrice Louvet and @ArtBasel's Noah Horowitz at @Semafor World Economy to examine what’s working in luxury.
🎙️ Listen now #linkinbio #TheBoFPodcast
📷 Getty Images, Semafor

posts.caption:
“I could never do the ordinary,” Joan Burstein told Colin McDowell back in 2014. Indeed, ordinary was never in the cards for the legendary Mrs B, who died on Friday at the age of 100. For more than half a century, she presided over Browns, a once small London fashion boutique that built a global reputation for championing daring talents like John Galliano, Alexander McQueen and Hussein Chalayan early in their careers, and bringing then new American brands like Ralph Lauren and Donna Karan to the UK market.
Read Colin McDowell’s profile of Joan Burstein: “Joan Burstein, Queen B.” #linkinbio
📷 Michael Hemy for BoF

posts.caption:
This week in fashion: Kering’s strategy overhaul; The return of Barneys — plus other key industry moves.
1. US health secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. said health regulators will ease rules on peptides, sparking a rally for wellness companies like Hims & Hers.
2. @AmericanEagle shares jumped after the retailer unveiled its second campaign featuring @Sydney_Sweeney, a year after an initial campaign that fuelled sales but polarised consumers.
3. @WMag announced the launch of @WYouth, a biannual print title for Gen Z readers helmed by Ava Nirui (@avanope) and editor-in-chief @SaraMoonves.
4. @DolceGabbana's leveraged business model has allowed the label to remain independent but is being tested by the luxury slowdown. Co-founder Stefano Gabbana is reportedly considering selling his 40 percent stake.
5. Licensing giant Authentic Brands Group is bringing @BarneysNY back to life at its previous flagship location at 660 Madison Avenue, sources said. Will it be the same?
6. @HalfMagicBeauty, the cosmetics brand from Euphoria makeup department head @Donni.Davy, debuted a Euphoria line to coincide with the premiere of the show’s third season, which has been widely panned.
7. @Kering_Official stock fell after the company detailed plans to double profit and close more @Gucci stores, but failed to provide revenue guidance beyond a vague promise to outperform the market.
8. @Hermès shares suffered a record plunge as Middle East sales slow and investors weigh the threat of overexposure, akin to what @LouisVuitton experienced in the early 2010s.
9. @Allbirds announced a head-scratching pivot from wool sneakers to AI, inviting suspicions that it’s all a ploy to chase tech hype and buoy its stock.
This Week in Fashion is BoF’s weekly news briefing, available exclusively to BoF Professional members. Become a BoF Professional member to access the full analysis #linkinbio

The star-studded event was attended by castmates Emily Blunt and @stanleytucci, as well as @ladygaga, @lucyliu, @cocorocha, @winnieharlow, @simoneashley and @ashleygraham.
Hathaway and Streep set the tone with a devilish color palette that spanned scarlet red, coral and black. Hathaway’s silk bustier gown was a custom @louisvuitton creation, while Streep’s cape coat was @givenchy. Streep, who returns to the screen to reprise her role as the inimitable Runway editor Miranda Priestly, made the look all the more commanding with a pair of black leather opera gloves and blacked-out shades.

posts.caption:
For teens during the decade, soundtrack albums were must-have merchandise, PARENTAL ADVISORY labels be damned.
The same year as @bazluhrmann's "Romeo + Juliet," "Trainspotting" came out and @iggypopofficial thumping "Lust for Life" set the pace for another CD that was both achingly cool and a massive commercial success. This was the era of "Dangerous Minds" (1995), "Good Will Hunting" (1997), "Cruel Intentions" (1999), and many more great albums that those of a certain age remember as clearly as the movies themselves.
The "Romeo + Juliet" album itself peaked at No.2 on the Billboard 200 chart and went multi-platinum in several countries. Meanwhile, as Marius de Vries, co-music producer on the film points out, films in the '90s were "regularly spawning enormous hit singles." Think @whitneyhouston's "I Will Always Love You" ("The Bodyguard," 1992) or @celinedion's "My Heart Will Go On" (@titanicmovie, 1997).
In the next decade, the rise of online streaming and death of CDs marked the end of the film soundtrack as we knew it. And while the current wave of '90s nostalgia is partly cyclical, it also taps into a longing for a "much more unified mass culture," said @robharvilla, host of the podcast, "60 Songs That Explain the '90s." In the fragmented internet age, "you don't have that same feeling of unity, where everybody is listening to 'Smells Like Teen Spirit' or 'You Outta Know' at the exact same time."
Without a doubt, the '90s were "a bunch of very golden years," de Vries said. "At the time, we were just having a lot of fun, and I think possibly that was reflected in the music we were making."
📸 : Merrick Morton/20th Century Fox/Shutterstock/Moviestore Collection Ltd/Alamy Stock Photo/Ronald Grant/Everett Collection
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by CNN Style on April 20, 2026 tagging @bazluhrmann, and @clairedanes. May be an image of poster, portable cassette player and text that says 'THE '90S MOVIE SOUNDTRACK THA STILL MAKES MILLENNIAL HEARTS FLUTTER CNN'.

posts.caption:
Citlali Fabián has been named Photographer of the Year at the Sony World Photography Awards 2026, with the judging panel hailing her photo series "Bilha, Stories of my Sisters," which focuses on Indigenous activists and artists in southern Mexico.
Fabián, who is from the Yalalteca Indigenous community, "uses photography to explore ways of addressing identity and its connections with territory, migration, and community bonds," said the organizers in a statement.
Alongside Fabián, winners and finalists were chosen across various categories in the professional competition. They include Joy Saha in the architecture and design category; Santiago Mesa in the documentary projects category; and Isadora Romero in the environment category.
The competition, which is now in its 19th year, received more than 430,000 images from more than 200 countries and territories, according to the organizers.
A selection of images from the awards will be displayed at Somerset House in London from April 17 to May 4.
See more images at the link in our bio.
📸: Sony World Photography Awards/ Citlali Fabián; Seungho Kim; Gargi Sharma; Will Burrard-Lucas; Jean-Marc Caimi & Valentina Piccinni; Pablo Ramos; Dafna Talmor; Isadora Romero

posts.caption:
This spring, it's all about tradwife with a twist. For a recording of "Live with Kelly and Mark" in New York on Wednesday, @ellefanning wore a girlish iridescent tutu and a buttery yellow polo shirt. From the waist down, she was dressed in her shimmering Sunday best.
From the waist up, the look was slouchy and boy-ish — like an off-duty tennis player, or someone's golf-loving father. The worlds-colliding look was designed by Paris-based brand August Barron, helmed by August Vestbø and Benjamin Barron, who subvert feminine and housewife aesthetics through unexpected layering, silhouettes and cutouts. Or often just by adding big black leather boots.
Fanning is currently promoting the Apple TV show "Margo's Got Money Troubles," and so far her outfits for the press tour have largely followed this line: slouchy, nostalgic and at times ironic versions of femininity. For her "Today" show appearance, Fanning wore the opening look from @rabanne Fall-Winter 2026 collection, which was inspired by tea dresses from the 1940s. The pinafore-style dress was in a twee ditsy print and flowed well past her knees, while a leather belt sat happily on her waist without exerting effort to cinch. On Monday, Fanning slipped into an equally vintage-inspired @albertaferretti rose-colored satin dress, covered in even more florals, with a built-in neckerchief.
📸: XNY/Star Max/GC Images/Getty Images; Cindy Ord/Getty Images; Jose Perez/Bauer-Griffin/GC Images/Getty Images

posts.caption:
Nina Hagen is a German singer, songwriter, and actress who first appeared in East Berlin before rising to fame in the late 1970s punk scene. She became known for her extreme voice, theatrical performances, and completely unpredictable public image. Early on she worked as an actress and later moved into music, where she mixed punk, opera, and performance art in a way nobody else was really doing at the time. She quickly became one of the most recognizable and unconventional figures in European punk culture and is still often called the “Godmother of Punk.” ￼
What makes Nina Hagen stand out is not just her music, but her personality. She has always been loud, expressive, and completely unfiltered, often mixing political, spiritual, and social ideas into her public presence. She never really fit into one category, which is exactly what made her interesting in the first place. ￼
In terms of feminism and fashion, Nina Hagen became an early symbol of anti-conformity. In a scene that was already breaking rules, she pushed it even further by using her body, voice, and style as a form of resistance. Punk fashion in general was about rejecting mainstream beauty standards, and she embodied that fully with exaggerated looks, bold makeup, and constantly changing identities. ￼
Her impact on feminism isn’t about slogans, but about presence. She showed that a woman in music didn’t have to be polished, quiet, or “acceptable” to be powerful. Her entire aesthetic and attitude became part of a wider movement where women in punk were not just performers, but central forces shaping the culture itself.
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on April 21, 2026. May be an image of text that says 'The Godmother of ofPunk, Nina Hagen. CULT'.

posts.caption:
In Tim Walker’s “boy/girl/boy” editorial for Vogue Italia (July 2016), fashion stops behaving like fashion in the traditional sense and becomes something more fluid, almost cinematic.
Stella Tennant and James Crewe are placed in this soft, slightly surreal world where gender doesn’t feel defined in a strict way. Instead, it’s all about mood, attitude, and the way clothes sit on the body. Tailoring shifts between sharp and relaxed, masculine and feminine blends without ever feeling forced or explained.
What stands out is how calm it all is. Nothing feels like a statement piece or a big idea being pushed. It’s more subtle than that. You just get the sense that the usual rules around dressing aren’t really important here.
Tim Walker builds a visual story that feels almost like a dream. It doesn’t try to be current or trend-driven, which is probably why it still feels so relevant today. It just exists in its own space and lets the clothes and people speak for themselves.
Credits:
Photography: Tim Walker
Styling: Jacob K
Models: Stella Tennant, James Crewe
Makeup: Miranda Joyce
Hair: Shon
Editorial: “boy/girl/boy”, Vogue Italia, July 2016
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on April 20, 2026. May be an image of overcoat, suit, magazine and text that says 'The Moment Gender in Fashion became Irrelevant. CULT'.

posts.caption:
Almost-forgotten couple Jamie Dornan and Keira Knightley once moved through the early 2000s like a quiet fashion statement.
Keira, just 18 and already the rising muse of Joe Wright, was stepping into global fame while Jamie was still working as a model, years away from his breakout in Fifty Shades of Grey.
But together they had this „easy-going“, almost cinematic presence. No loud headlines, no overdone drama, just that soft early 2000s cool that somehow defined the mood of an entire era.
Keira’s style at the time felt like the blueprint. Minimal, sharp, slightly undone. Jamie matched it with that clean, luxury model aesthetic, like he belonged in every black and white campaign of that decade.
They weren’t trying to be iconic, but somehow they became it. A couple that felt more like a vibe than a storyline, shaping how that generation saw beauty, love and even fashion itself.
It was short lived, two years that ended without scandal or chaos, just distance and timing. But the influence of that era they created together still lingers in every “effortless cool” reference that came after.
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on April 20, 2026. May be a meme of one or more people, hair and text that says 'The Couple that shaped α whole Era.. CULT®'.

posts.caption:
The editorial “Mad About You” (Vogue US, 2003), reflects the early-2000s shift toward emotional, story-driven fashion imagery.
Rather than focusing only on clothing, the editorial builds a mood around obsession, romance, and identity. Like many Vogue productions of that time, it treats fashion as a narrative space—where models become characters and each image feels cinematic.
The result is a stylized visual story that blends glamour with attitude, capturing the feeling of being completely consumed by style and emotion.
Magazine : Vogue US
Photographer : Steven Meisel
Make Up: Pat McGrath
Hair : Garren
Styling : Camilla Nickerson
Model : Stella Tennant & Eugenia Volodina
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on April 20, 2026. May be an image of one or more people, makeup, hair, magazine and text that says 'This is what Editorials should look like. CULT®'.

posts.caption:
The style of Depeche Mode was never fixed, it evolved like their sound, moving from playful synth-pop „innocence“ to something darker, more cinematic, and ultimately iconic !
In their early years, their look was almost anti-fashion: slightly awkward, “nerd-chic,” with bomber jackets, ties, wool sweaters, and a kind of everyday British normality. It matched their bright, experimental synth-pop phase - quirky, accessible, and a bit naive.
By the mid-80s, they moved into something more provocative and refined: leather, monochrome fits, and subtle androgyny. With the visual direction of Anton Corbijn, their aesthetic became cinematic, stripped back, shadowy, and instantly recognizable.
This is where their style turns timeless: not trend-driven, but mood-driven. Black becomes a signature, silhouettes become sharper, and every look feels intentional without trying too hard.
More than fashion, Depeche Mode created an atmosphere that is still unmatched until today.
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on April 19, 2026. May be an image of magazine, poster and text that says 'Depeche Mode: Style with Substance! CULT'.

posts.caption:
The first step into luxury may be changing.
For decades, diamonds defined the entry into fine jewellery. Today, evolving consumer behaviour is reshaping that journey. With growing demand for individuality, accessible price points and design-led purchases, coloured gemstones are increasingly becoming part of the entry-level luxury conversation.
For retailers, this is not about replacement — but about understanding how the customer journey is expanding.
Save this for your retail strategy.

posts.caption:
The fashions seen over the last two Coachella weekends were far from the eccentric, highly curated, culturally-appropriative bohemian glam looks we expected to see. In fact, they were the total opposite.
Stars like Kendall Jenner, Hailey Baldwin, Amber Rose, and Vanessa Hudgens wrote the playbook for how to dress at Coachella: Feathers in your hair, tribal headdresses and flower crowns, macrame crop-tops, and Navajo prints. Coachella style was deeply problematic, it was plastered all over Tumblr, and it was so codified that it became its own retail category: the annual “festival collection.”
But now, the same celebs are turning against it. Kendall Jenner was seen this year in basic tank tops and baggy jorts. Katy Perry and Justin Trudeau’s white t-shirt stunned the internet. And Hailey Beiber, the first lady of Beiberchella, wore only a vintage windbreaker one weekend, and a hoodie with running shorts the next weekend.
There is a lot influencing this: We spotted indie sleaze style reflected in Gabbriette’s Chrome Hearts looks. Amber Rose’s shorts, Hailey Bieber’s and Emma Chamberlain’s leather boots, and Amelia Gray’s stiff straight-cut denim all echoed fashion’s growing interest in workwear. And, the quiet luxury movement informed almost all these outfits being that each came with surreptitious designer tags.
But also, this movement toward underdressing took place under the giant YouTube screen of Beiberchella, which was, by the way, similarly minimal and ad hoc. Erego, this isn’t just a fashion issue (it never is!) but is indicative of a larger trend that is skeptical of overproduction and is nostalgic for authenticity. Perhaps it’s a foil to the onslaught of product placements, highly styled appearances, and manufactured moments that have overtaken Coachella.
Celebrity underdressers appear to be offering counterpoint, a way to distance themselves from the masses who are clawing for visibility at music fests. Or perhaps they’re reaching for the styles that made music festivals so iconic in the first place, back when it was common to be a little messy. Dirty, even. A la Kate Moss at Glastonbury. Is Coachella underdressing actually a breath of fresh air?
posts.alt_text: Photo by Diet Prada ™ on April 20, 2026. May be an image of text that says 'RIP Overdressing at Coachella Celebrities and seasoned Coachella-goers embraced underdressing in contrast to their styling in former years. Diet PRADA'.

posts.caption:
Hallmark Channel dropped the promo poster for its new movie ‘All’s Fair in Love and Mahjong’ on Friday and the internet wasted no time dragging it.
For a movie centered around mahjong, people are side-eyeing the casting choices. The poster shows a mostly white cast, with just one Asian actor, Yan-Kay Crystal Lowe, who is of Chinese-Scottish descent.
The rom-com synopsis reads: “A school nurse turns her love of the game Mahjong into a new path, inspired by a jovial contractor fixing up her home, who encourages her to take a chance on the future.”
Meanwhile, the mahjong tiles featured on the poster are from the film’s branded set, which Hallmark plans to sell on its website through a collab with Miss Mahjong. The Kansas-based game company says it was founded by “a trio of Mahjong-obsessed friends and neighbors” named Anne Marie, Morgan, and Betsy. Although the founders don’t share additional details (🧐) the optics are giving the same energy as a the whitewashed mahjong brands we previously called out: Oh My Mahjong! (just three weeks ago) and The Mahjong Line back in 2021.
The film’s title, a spin on the phrase, “all’s fair in love and war,” was probably meant to be cheeky, but instead the comments section seems ready for battle with the network.
“Just do monopoly next time or something,” wrote one person. “Collective ancestral sigh,” wrote another.
To top it off, the movie is set to premiere May 9… during AAPI Heritage Month. @hallmarkchannel it’s not too late to scrap this mess.
#mahjong #movie #film #aapi #viral

posts.caption:
California’s race for governor is undergoing a major political shakeup after Eric Swalwell’s campaign imploded almost overnight, going from leading candidate to disgraced ex-congressman. Multiple women have accused him of sexual assault and harassment, which he denies. With a criminal investigation now hanging over him, Swalwell dropped out Monday and resigned from Congress, leaving the top spot suddenly wide open.
There are still seven Dems in the running, including Xavier Becerra, Tony Thurmond, Betty Yee, Matt Mahan, and Antonio Villaraigosa. But right now, the frontrunner is looking like climate activist Tom Steyer who is leading with 21% in a SurveyUSA poll conducted last week as Swalwell’s scandal broke, followed by former Rep. Katie Porter at 8%.
On the campaign trail last week, Steyer pointed out gas in downtown LA reached $8.29/gal, adding, “The working people of California are paying for Donald Trump’s war in Iran.”
Apparently that hit a nerve. By Monday, Trump was throwing (another) tantrum on Truth Social. “Why is FoxNews showing SLEAZEBAG Tom Steyer, doing a big piece on him right now, and probably putting him “into play,” instead of talking about Republican Candidates, and really good ones like Steve Hilton, and Republicans?,” he whined.
“Nobody cares about Tom Steyer, he is a LOSER, but Fox is making it possible for a guy like this to win. VOTE FOR STEVE HILTON, AND TURN CALIFORNIA AROUND!”
The GOP hasn’t had a CA governor since Schwarzenegger left in 2011, but they see a potential opening as Steve Hilton stands at 18%, according to the same poll.
Under the state’s top-two primary system, the two candidates with the most votes will run in November, regardless of party. So yes, it’s still anyone’s race and the June 2 primary is around the corner. Mark your calendars!
#california #news

posts.caption:
Pete Hegseth quoted a ‘Pulp Fiction’ monologue like it was scripture during a Pentagon prayer service on Wednesday, citing the iconic speech hitman Jules (Samuel L. Jackson) delivers before his victims get whacked.
“They call it CSAR 25:17, which I think is meant to reflect Ezekiel 25:17,” the Secretary of War said. “It reads — and pray with me please — ‘the path of the downed aviator is beset on all sides by the inequities of the selfish and the tyranny of evil men.”
“Blessed is he who in the name of camaraderie and duty shepherds the lost through the valley of darkness, for he is truly his brother’s keeper and the finder of lost children. And I will strike down upon thee with great vengeance and furious anger those who attempt to capture and destroy my brother, and you will know my call sign is Sandy 1 when I lay my vengeance upon thee. Amen.”
In reality, Ezekiel 25:17 is much more brief. “And I will execute great vengeance upon them with furious rebukes; and they shall know that I am the Lord, when I shall lay my vengeance upon them.”
Welp, what’s his next speech going to lift from? Betting on ‘Braveheart.’
#pulpfiction #prayer #news #reels #threads

Cause I've been blastin and laughin so long that even my momma thinks that my mind is gone..."'.

posts.caption:
This week, Massimo Dutti unveils their Limited Edition Spring/Summer ‘26 collection with an immersive retail experience in Paris. To highlight the collection, the brand has collaborated with a handful of artisans and tastemakers from an array of practices spanning culinary, visual, and literary arts.
While the pop-up features apparel, Massimo Dutti has brought an eye-catching roster of collaborators to compliment the collection. Galerie Jacques Lacoste and Galerie Patrick Seguin create an aesthetically precise and artistically fluid space, imbued with the sophistication of the brand. Librairie Yvon Lambert, the Parisian art bookstore beloved by the coolest girl you follow on IG, supplies an editorial perspective within a photo-worthy reading nook, to be enjoyed alongside coffee by the chicest of Parisian coffee shops, Café Nuances.
Of course, the grand opening will be enviably stylish as well with Chef Rose Chalalai supplying a culinary angle for the evening, and florist Nina Charles will punctuate the space with her minimalistic yet moody arrangements.
Visitors will have the opportunity to shop the Spring/Summer ‘26 collection, which is populated by sophisticated staple pieces that are as refined and elegant as they are relaxed and utterly versatile. Languid trousers pair effortlessly with cotton linen silk tops. Thoughtfully draped dresses and skirts beg to be worn on warm summer days and nights. And the collection is anchored by a selection of timeless leather statement pieces in Nappa leather and sheepskin suede.
Massimo Dutti’s immersive approach reflects retail’s larger shift toward experiential offerings that don’t just give customers the opportunity to shop, but demonstrates a brand’s total value proposition in terms of lifestyle, culture, community, and enrichment. The space gracefully aligns Massimo Dutti with contemporary arts and culture pillars at a time when similar brands are grasping to engage in new ways with their customers while appealing to an increasingly upmarket clientele. The pop up will take place from April 17th and 27th at 7 Rue Froissart.

posts.caption:
Bhanu Athaiya had the girls’ “baddie” ready half a century ago. The modern imagination of the medieval Indian attire is hugely made up of Athaiya’s pioneering work in Bollywood movies. Athaiya reintroduced the seductress, the showgirl, the divine feminine, and the va va voom, from her extensive knowledge— constructing a visual grammar that has morphed into the blueprint of what sexy could look like in Indian attire. A reference of all references. The same style is now incorporated in the desi-fication of music festival attire. Temple jewellary. Cropped blouses. Mini saris.
The invention of the style had been done centuries ago; the difference is that what was an identity carved in stone, then brought back through meticulous research, has now come back as mere poly-blend aesthetic.

posts.caption:
#ELLECoverStar: There are artists who arrive with noise, and then there are artists like Zayn Malik (@zayn), the kind who never really needed to raise his voice to command attention. Some people have presence; Malik has always had pull. Fifteen years after his debut, and nearly a decade into his solo career, Malik returns to ELLE India, where he first made history in 2018 with his first-ever Indian magazine cover. There’s no forced “full circle” narrative here, no overworked nostalgia. If anything, this moment feels less like a return and more like a continuation — the same artist, only more settled into himself. “I don’t feel like I’m at such a different place in my career,” he says. “I feel like I’m at a different place and point as a human; I’m developing, growing, and learning from life experiences.” That distinction feels important. The artistry was never in question. If anything, it is the life around it that now feels more fully aligned. Head to the 🔗in bio to read the full conversation with our cover star.

posts.caption:
#ELLEDigitalCoverStar: The thing about Karan Tacker (@karantacker) is that he has never been just one version of himself. For many, he lives rent-free in a particular corner of Indian television history: Sameer Saxena from ‘Love Ne Mila Di Jodi,’ his 2009 debut, which opened the door to ‘Ek Hazaaron Mein Meri Behna Hai’ and gave the country Viren Singh Vadhera. He was that man — the one you rooted for, the one you watched through a softer lens — and he was very good at it. On a Zoom call, quietly edging towards forty, he has simply kept moving. Add to that a business he’s been building in the margins of it all. The romantic hero didn’t disappear; he just had more to say.

posts.caption:
@oliviadeano is everywhere—and for good reason.
Since the September release of her hit sophomore album, ‘The Art of Loving,’ the British songstress has been riding a serious wave of momentum on both sides of the pond. Add in a Grammy win, standout live performances, a @cartier campaign, and critical raves, and it’s clear: Dean’s starpower is undeniable. As she prepares to kick off the album’s sold-out world tour, Dean is trying her best to soak it all in—and define success on her own terms.
In ELLE’s Women in Music cover story, @sylviaobell chats with the Grammy winner about navigating public opinion in her newfound spotlight, the wisdom the women in her life have imparted on her, and the precedent she hopes to set with her upcoming tour: “I don’t think you need to be someone who’s got loads of money to enjoy your favorite album.” Read the full interview at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
The beauty industry's newest discovery? Gen X.
A few years after the rise of the Gen Alpha baby-beauty junkie, brands are moving on to decidedly more 40-and-over pastures. Just look at @lorealparis partnering with @gilliana, @itcosmetics signing @jenniegarth, or @pamelaanderson co-founding @sonsieskin; theirs is the generation predicted to spend the most on skin care and makeup compared to all others, after all. Thanks to the combined impact of Gen X women's changing beauty needs as well as their increasingly active online activity, the so-called “middle child” of generations is finally being seen by an industry that has historically only marketed to twentysomethings or 70-year-olds with gray hair.
“I’ve never heard Gen X more than I hear it now,” says one 49-year-old content creator. ”We are spending more money and time on ourselves. We’re taking that trip. We’re just feeling emboldened to just embrace and live life. These are beautiful years.”
Tap the link in bio to read the full story.

posts.caption:
#EmilyBlunt celebrated the debut of ‘The Devil Wears Prada 2’ in style—and showed up last night dripping in pearls and diamonds for her night out in New York.
Wearing a selection of designs from Mikimoto’s “Les Pétales Place Vendôme Rosés” collection, Blunt says she “immediately fell in love” with the brand’s choker the moment she saw it. “Such a special piece with the most exquisite detailing and design,” she tells ELLE. Blunt’s jewelry look came punctuated with a series of stunning pieces—including the 18K rose gold choker, bracelet, and earrings. “I’ve long admired the Maison, and was truly honored to wear such an extraordinary creation that reflects a remarkable heritage.”
Head to the link in bio for more details on her look for the world premiere of the long-awaited sequel.

posts.alt_text: Photo by Fashion is Psychology™️ on March 19, 2025.

posts.caption:
“Hi Shakaila we’re xyz producers and we’d love to make a tv show with you” I’ve had a handful of those conversations over the years and they quickly go from 🤩 to 🫩
I used to feel disheartened until I looked around and saw so many examples of people not waiting for permission and instead deciding to get up and make shit happen! So that’s what I decided to do, turn this decades long dream into a reality! I pulled in every favour I had! Turned my friends into producers and show runners, begged and borrowed and you know what, it turned out pretty damn good if I do say so myself 😅
This black history month especially, I’m inspired by the talented Black British women who have shown me that it’s possible to make a mark on the small screen no matter the limitations!
I absolutely loved makeover shows growing up! But as I got older I saw how they were more brutal than beautiful!
Today, fashion psychology shows us just how deeply our clothes impact our mental health, identity, and confidence.
That’s why I’m so excited to share The Wearapy Show my new makeover series that blends fashion psychology, emotional healing and real style transformations.
We help everyday people reclaim their wardrobes after major life shifts: grief, motherhood, identity struggles, and more.
With a team of experts and no judgment, just science-backed guidance and love.
Episodes 1 and 2 launch 26th October on the Fashion is Psychology YouTube Channel. Subscribe now and follow the journey.
#bhm #wearapy #fashionpsychology #makeover

posts.caption:
HUGE NEWS: Good On You has officially rated over 7,000 brands 🎉 This is a major accomplishment, as even more brands—from the small and indie through the large and luxury—are in our Directory.⁠
⁠
What goes into ratings? 🤔 A lot, actually: we have a rigorous, comprehensive system analysing up to 1,000 data points across 100 key issues to arrive at our lowest score (“We Avoid”) to our highest score (“Great”). Ratings are based solely on a brand’s transparent, public reporting that we pair with credible third-party indices, certifications, and standards.⁠
⁠
How do we select brands to rate? Well…with your help 🥹 Many of our ratings come through community requests, shared in comments and messages, which helps us understand which brands to prioritise to meet this moment. Additionally, our retail and ecommerce partners along with brands request ratings too, which is a key means to apply change to the industry from within.⁠
⁠
Another item to celebrate here? Of these rated brands, 1,000 have received our two highest scores, “Good” and “Great”. This is very exciting news, representative of how more brands are doing considering their impact on workers, the planet, and animals. (Even if that also means…6000 brands have a lot more to do to reduce their impact.)⁠
⁠
Here’s to continuing to keep people like you informed while supporting more sustainable brands that are making the difference! Cheers 🥂 Explore our 7,000 ratings at the link too ✨⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #SustainableStyle #ConsciousStyle #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion⁠
posts.alt_text: Photo by Good On You: sustainability ratings on January 21, 2026.

posts.caption:
Meet the top rated brands of 2025 🎊⁠
⁠
In the past year, we rated more than 1500 brands and are proud to share the ten that received our highest marks, illustrating a commitment to more sustainable practices that protect people, the planet, and animals 💚⁠
⁠
What’s a theme that unites these brands? 👀 Outside of a commitment to reducing their impact, these brands are all small and independent, a reminder of where to look in the industry for leadership and innovation. If you’re looking for a reminder to support small brands this year, this is it.⁠
⁠
Cheers to all these brands, along with all the brands that were rated “Good” and “Great” in the past year! Here’s to continuing to make a difference and for championing a better approach to what we wear. Find the full list at the link—and share which brands you’re hoping to see rated in 2026 in the comments 💬⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #SustainableBeauty #EcoBeauty #Beauty #BeautyBrand #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion

posts.caption:
What can be accomplished in ten years?⁠
⁠
A decade may seem short but, in reflecting on all the work we’ve done in the past decade, we’re in awe of everything that has transpired—and are grateful for the hard work of the current and former team members along with you.⁠
⁠
Whether you’ve shared our posts or have been a partner in this business, the work of creating a more sustainable future isn’t done alone but in partnership with others. These accomplishments are an example of what happens when likeminded people come together in the hopes of a better world 🌍🌎🌏⁠
⁠
Swipe to learn about ten things Good On You has accomplished in the past ten years. Share how you think the sustainable fashion and beauty industry have shifted in the past decade 💚⁠

posts.caption:
This is *not* an easy time for smaller, more sustainable brands 😭⁠
⁠
From steep tariffs to economic precarity, retailers skipping payments to fast fashion running prices down, upholding good values in challenging times is indeed a challenge—and small brands are feeling the pressure.⁠
⁠
To have a more sustainable fashion industry, more sustainable fashion has to be supported both by the industry and by consumers. This is why we always say to support top rated brands, shop circularly, and to speak out about your choices, all to turn away from brands whose values are making the survival of sustainability difficult 💔⁠
⁠
How are you navigating this moment, as a shopper, brand, or industry professional? Share in the comments, offering advice or to vent, all in the hopes of supporting the community. Visit the link to find an explainer on the costs of making more sustainable fashion ↑⁠
⁠

posts.caption: The last thing Emma Grede wants to do is lie to you. That’s why her debut book, “Start With Yourself,” isn’t your average business read. Instead of a self-help book or motivational story, Grede’s is a practical how-to for kindred, enterprising spirits. Business books can be a crowded market, and there is certainly no shortage of writers trying to peddle silver-bullet solutions for elusive success—but few offer the specific vantage point that Grede does. She is the modern embodiment of the word “mogul”—see her recent appearance on #TheGoodBuy podcast for proof. At the link in our bio, she tells Bazaar exactly what makes this project different from all the others and unpacks what like-minded women can learn from her story.

posts.caption:
“You smell good” is arguably one of the best compliments you can get—and with the first-ever cologne by @escentric_molecules you’ll be sure to show up on everyone’s radar. ⁠
⁠
Challenging the norms, Cologne One distills everything Escentric Molecules is known for: innovation, radical minimalism, and a true perfume-lover appeal. Turning simplicity into a minimalist work of art from the first spritz, the scent encapsulates that first sip of a gin and tonic, sparkling notes of lime, lemon, and bergamot meet the herbal kick of juniper, softened by a touch of ginger and crisp cucumber. At its base, Iso E Super, Ambroxan, and a whisper of musk linger in a way that feels almost personal. Genderless, understated, and boundary-pushing, Cologne One leaves an impression, capturing the spirit of someone unafraid to challenge conventions and celebrate simplicity in a world of excess.⁠
⁠
Hit the link in bio to find out more.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Highsnobiety Style #HSStyle on April 21, 2026. May be an image of fragrance, perfume, magazine, poster and text that says 'A FRESH TAKE: ESCENTRIC MOLECULES' FIRST EVER COLOGNE Η'.

posts.caption:
@a.presse_ isn’t for everyone. Except, occasionally, when it is.⁠
⁠
The famously secretive Japanese label, known for its lack of a website, limited runs, and high barrier to entry, rarely collaborates. But its ongoing partnership with @everyone.tokyo, a modest brand refining classic Americana staples, remains a notable exception. Their latest April 18 drop introduces silk-linen “Sleeping Shirts” and “Sleeping Pants”: loose, black, subtly lustrous pieces that reinterpret traditional pajamas as elevated everyday wear, staying true to A.PRESSE’s quiet fabric experimentation.⁠
⁠
While A.PRESSE typically reserves special projects for select retailers, EVERYONE stands as its only consistent collaborator. The duo’s growing catalog, from gabardine suiting to heritage hoodies and cashmere shirting, has become one of menswear’s most low-key yet compelling partnerships.

posts.caption:
A kid with scraps and a vision is out here outdressing the algorithm.
@kaluputics a young East African designer, is turning everyday materials into striking, original fits that feel anything but ordinary. From reworked uniforms to bold, styled looks, his creativity stretches far beyond his resources, proving raw talent doesn't wait for permission.
What makes it even sharper is how he's using social media as his runway, pushing his work to a global audience and catching real momentum.
If he already has this level of eye and execution with limited tools, imagine what happens when the right support finds him...

posts.caption:
@thenorthface is making outdoor gear that actually works for more people.⁠
⁠
Its new Universal Collection takes a fresh, inclusive approach to camping essentials, built with input from adaptive athletes Vasu Sojitra and Maureen Beck. The focus is on simple, intuitive design that makes gear easier and more comfortable to use, no matter your experience level. Think a zipperless sleeping bag, a more user-friendly tent, a free-standing day pack, plus a convertible hat and interchangeable camp slippers. ⁠
⁠
The collection is all about lowering barriers and getting more people outside.

posts.caption:
For Milan Design Week, @louisvuitton unleashed a 1920s time capsule of a collection at Palazzo Serbelloni — featuring a reissue of its first-ever piece. 💯⁠
⁠
Inspired by the brand's Art Deco era, the latest Objets Nomades collection is broken down into themed living spaces. The various rooms, from the Giangaleazzo room to the Grand Foyer, stand as a tribute to Pierre Legrain (1888–1929), who designed the first Louis Vuitton furniture line. ⁠
⁠
Legrain’s debut piece, the Celeste dressing table, is reissued in its original Omega shape with lacquered wood and Nomade leather. Elsewhere, the Riviera Chilienne chair is resurrected from the Legrain archives, boasting wood, leather, and mother-of-pearl marquetry. In another room occupying a period-style train car, rare artefacts, including Louis Vuitton’s earliest trunks and travel accessories, form a dialogue with the new Objets Nomades collection.⁠
⁠
The Louis Vuitton exhibition at Palazzo Serbelloni is open to the public from 21 to 26 April 2026, from 10 am to 8 pm. ⁠
⁠
Photo: Louis Vuitton
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by HYPEBEAST on April 21, 2026 tagging @louisvuitton. May be an image of text that says 'HYPEBEAST Louis Vuitton's First Furniture Piece Is Back After 100 Years'.

Summer has a mood for every version of you—and @nidhhiagerwal knows exactly how to wear each one. Whether it’s the soft, golden haze of a slow morning or the unapologetic heat of high noon, she shifts between them the way only someone truly at ease in her own skin can. 🌸
In collaboration with @aurelia_womenswear , this becomes more than a seasonal study—it is a portrait of summer as emotion, texture, and transformation.
Light changes, moods shift, and she moves through it all with the kind of effortless cool that makes you want to rethink your entire wardrobe.
From dewy and dreamy to bold and sun-kissed, this is summer dressing as self-expression—textured, emotional, and completely her own.✨

His breakthrough as Balram in The White Tiger earned a BAFTA nomination, and he’s since transformed across roles, from Kho Gaye Hum Kahan to Superboys of Malegaon and Alien: Earth.
Now, with Tu Yaa Main alongside Shanaya Kapoor, Gourav proves one thing… wherever the story goes, he makes it impossible to look away. We step into the dreamer and shapeshifter’s world for our April cover.

posts.caption:
Man of the Moment — Yashasvi Jaiswal (@yashasvijaiswal28) continues to rise, and we’re here for every second of it.
From dreaming of centuries in blue to turning those visions into reality and now hitting it out of the park as the opener for Rajasthan Royal (@rajasthanroyals) in IPL, his journey is the very definition of hard work meeting opportunity. Powered as much by an unshakable mindset as by his brilliance on the pitch, our cover star opens up about the drive, discipline, and hunger that fuel his remarkable ascent, “I don’t know where I’m going to go, but I want to enjoy the journey.”
Starting out as a cricketing prodigy, now 24, he’s all about keeping his head in the game, stopping every now and then, to savour the simple joys of life. And maybe that is the secret sauce to success, one game at a time.
Here’s raising a toast to the ‘player of the match’- Yashasvi Jaiswal!

posts.caption:
Introducing our latest cover star, @katehudson!
It’s been over 25 years since Kate Hudson earned her first Oscar nomination for her breakout role as Penny Lane in ‘Almost Famous,’ and she’s been shaping the cultural moment ever since. Despite her success—and her roots in Hollywood royalty—she’s remained remarkably grounded, continuing to speak about the industry with a genuine sense of gratitude.
“I just really want to make movies that people love,” Hudson tells L’OFFICIEL. “It’s really simple for me. I feel a responsibility as someone that loves this business, and grew up in it, and really wanted to make movies. I hope that I can make movies and shows that people really want to see.”
That, of course, has always been Hudson’s strength. Fresh off another Oscar nomination for her role in ‘Song Sung Blue,’ she’s now turning her attention back to television with Season 2 of ‘Running Point.’ In addition to executive producing the Netflix series, Hudson stars as Isla Gordon, a character loosely inspired by Los Angeles Lakers owner and president Jeanie Buss. While the role has prompted comparisons to Hudson’s own upbringing, she’s quick to dismiss that narrative. “Jeanie has had to maintain the most important team in sports history, and on top of that, the money and the franchise and everything that goes with it,” she says. “For me, we’re just actors. People are either gonna like what we do or they’re not.”
Read our full interview with Hudson about the collaborative nature of film, her creative process, and growing up in the industry at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Cento anni non basterebbero a contenere Marilyn.
Dal 21 aprile al 24 giugno, @Mudec_Official ospita Beyond The Icon – Marilyn Monroe: A Centennial Tribute by GUESS, una mostra immersiva che attraversa fotografia, cinema e moda per raccontare la donna oltre il mito. Tre sguardi, tre fotografi — Bernard of Hollywood, Sam Shaw, Milton H. Greene — e un’unica protagonista che continua a definire cosa significa essere un’icona. #MilanDesignWeek
Per @Guess non è solo un tributo: il brand ha dedicato a Marilyn il suo primo paio di jeans, e da allora il suo immaginario non ha mai smesso di influenzare le campagne, le collezioni, il DNA stesso del marchio. La nuova #MarilynMonroe Capsule Collection Summer 2026 è la traduzione contemporanea di questo legame — che dura da sempre e non ha intenzione di finire.
#BeyondTheIcon #GUESS

posts.caption:
Durante la nostra conversazione, diventa subito chiaro che per Kate Hudson non si tratta affatto di frasi di circostanza. Hudson osserva davvero i suoi colleghi, li studia, ne assorbe il metodo e la sensibilità. Cita il discorso di Harrison Ford al ricevimento del suo Lifetime Achievement Award agli Actors Awards, richiama una riflessione di Kristen Stewart sull’attore e sulla recitazione come atto intrinsecamente legato alla sottomissione, parla con ammirazione di Ethan Hawke e del modo in cui sostiene i giovani talenti, e si illumina raccontando la sua co-protagonista in Song Sung Blue, Ella Anderson, lodandone la capacità di costruire un personaggio dall’interno.
Per Hudson, ogni scambio con un altro attore è un’opportunità di apprendimento. «È bello parlare con le persone di ciò che dà loro la sicurezza per fare un certo passo. Ognuno di noi ha qualcosa che gli viene naturale, e ciò che per me è semplice potrebbe non esserlo per qualcun altro. Mi piace osservare il modo in cui gli altri trovano il proprio percorso. Lo vedo anche in mio figlio. Ci vuole tempo, e la verità è che non si smette mai di imparare».
@katehudson

posts.caption:
@KateHudson has taken on a wide range of genres, but she’s still focused on pushing herself even further. When we asked Hudson what she still hopes to achieve in her career, she didn’t point to accolades—just a desire to keep pursuing roles that scare her, the same approach she brings to everything she does. ⁠
⁠
“I enter every experience like it’s new, like I don’t know how it’s going to unfold,” she explains. “I come in doing my part, which is understanding what makes me feel confident in the role and the character, but I do that so that when I’m there, I can be flexible, not rigid.” Read Hudson’s full interview at the link in our bio. ⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
Hollywood couldn’t ask for a better team player than @katehudson. She may have been the one to earn a Best Actress Oscar nomination for her portrayal in ‘Song Sung Blue,’ but for Hudson, film is an inherently collaborative medium—and despite her individual accolades, it continues to be all about the ensemble. ⁠
⁠
“This moment doesn’t feel personal,” she tells L’OFFICIEL. “It feels like there are so many people along the way who are a part of it.” Read more from Hudson as she discusses the collaborative nature of the industry at the link in our bio. ⁠

posts.caption:
Introducing our latest cover star, @katehudson!
It’s been over 25 years since Kate Hudson earned her first Oscar nomination for her breakout role as Penny Lane in ‘Almost Famous,’ and she’s been shaping the cultural moment ever since. Despite her success—and her roots in Hollywood royalty—she’s remained remarkably grounded, continuing to speak about the industry with a genuine sense of gratitude.
“I just really want to make movies that people love,” Hudson tells L’OFFICIEL. “It’s really simple for me. I feel a responsibility as someone that loves this business, and grew up in it, and really wanted to make movies. I hope that I can make movies and shows that people really want to see.”
That, of course, has always been Hudson’s strength. Fresh off another Oscar nomination for her role in ‘Song Sung Blue,’ she’s now turning her attention back to television with Season 2 of ‘Running Point.’ In addition to executive producing the Netflix series, Hudson stars as Isla Gordon, a character loosely inspired by Los Angeles Lakers owner and president Jeanie Buss. While the role has prompted comparisons to Hudson’s own upbringing, she’s quick to dismiss that narrative. “Jeanie has had to maintain the most important team in sports history, and on top of that, the money and the franchise and everything that goes with it,” she says. “For me, we’re just actors. People are either gonna like what we do or they’re not.”
Read our full interview with Hudson about the collaborative nature of film, her creative process, and growing up in the industry at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
⁠While her approach to acting has always been instinctive and fluid, producing calls for a more structured mindset for @katehudson, who continues to expand her role behind the scenes as executive producer on ‘Running Point.’ ⁠
⁠
It’s also a natural next step in her career. Inspired by her mother, Goldie Hawn—one of the first actresses to produce her own films—Hudson explored producing early on, but stepped back after ‘Bride Wars,’ when she was a new mom and the timing didn’t feel right.⁠
⁠
As with everything else, she waited until she could fully commit—a quality that sets her up for a successful future as a producer. Read our full interview with Hudson at the link in our bio. ⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
Bridgerton isn’t just a romantic period drama, it’s one of Netflix’s most lavish productions. The series reportedly carries a staggering budget of around $168 million, placing it among the most expensive shows created by the streaming giant.
Each episode is estimated to cost around $7 million, funding the show’s extravagant costumes, grand sets, and cinematic storytelling that recreates Regency-era London. The scale of production is what gives the series its signature opulence and visual richness.
The spending doesn’t stop at sets and wardrobes. Reports also reveal impressive cast salaries, with some actors and voice talents earning up to $1 million for their roles, reflecting the show’s massive global success.
From lavish balls to royal romances, Bridgerton proves that luxury storytelling comes with a royal price tag and Netflix is willing to pay for it.
Images: Courtesy of Netflix
#Netflix #Luxurious

posts.caption:
Indian cricketer Jasprit Bumrah was recently seen wearing the ₹36-lakh @JacobAndCo Epic X ‘Sher-e-Punjab’ Edition, a distinctive timepiece that blends high-end watchmaking with strong cultural inspiration.
Created by Jacob & Co as a tribute to Sikh heritage, the watch features a lightweight 44mm titanium case and a skeletonised dial that reveals the intricate mechanics within. The vertical bridges are engraved with meaningful Sikh symbols, including the lion representing courage and leadership, the Khanda symbolising strength and unity, and the sacred Ek Onkar reflecting the belief in one universal God.
The titanium structure is paired with a bold blue honeycomb-textured rubber strap, a colour closely associated with Sikh identity. At its core is the hand-wound JCAM02 movement, offering a 48-hour power reserve while showcasing the watch’s detailed inner workings through the open dial.
Limited to just 50 pieces worldwide, the Sher-e-Punjab Edition honours the legacy of Guru Gobind Singh and the founding of the Khalsa, making it both a luxury watch and a tribute to heritage and resilience. ⌚✨

posts.caption:
Saint Laurent closes Spring 2026 with a finale that feels instantly iconic. The voluminous gowns have already taken over editorials, worn by Hailey Bieber and Kylie Jenner. In a season of excess, these silhouettes stand out as the defining fashion moment.
Designed by Anthony Vaccarello, the dresses are all about drama. Oversized ruffles, sweeping skirts, and bold, saturated colors create instant impact. They don’t just wear well, they photograph even better.
There’s also a clear nod to Yves Saint Laurent himself. The grandeur echoes his couture from the 1970s and 1980s, where volume and elegance went hand in hand. It’s heritage, reimagined for a new era of fashion storytelling.
Beyond aesthetics, there’s a smart practicality. Crafted in lightweight nylon, these gowns are easier to move, style and shoot. Which makes the question simple—who wore it best?
Images: @ysl
#SaintLaurent #LuxuryFashion #Luxurious

posts.caption:
Ralph Lauren is facing backlash, this time over a ₹44,800 skirt that many say feels unmistakably familiar. Marketed as a “Bandhani-inspired” wrap skirt, the design has sparked criticism across India, with conversations quickly centering on the absence of clear cultural acknowledgment. What was positioned as inspiration has instead ignited a wider debate around appropriation in luxury fashion.
At the core of the controversy is Bandhani, one of India’s oldest and most culturally significant textile crafts. Originating in the western states of Gujarat and Rajasthan, the technique involves intricate hand-tied resist dyeing, often used in garments tied to celebrations and ceremonial wear. Critics argue that reducing such a heritage-rich method to a loosely defined “tie-dye print” overlooks the depth of skill and cultural meaning embedded in the craft.
Much of the backlash focuses on recognition and authenticity. Observers noted that the skirt appears to feature printed fabric rather than hand-tied craftsmanship, yet is positioned at a luxury price point. The larger concern, many say, is the absence of any direct reference to India or acknowledgment of the artisan communities who have preserved this centuries-old technique.
The moment also highlights a broader shift across the global fashion industry. As consumers become more culturally aware, expectations around credit and collaboration have grown sharper. In today’s luxury landscape, creativity alone is no longer enough. Proper attribution, meaningful collaboration, and respect for origin are becoming essential to how heritage is presented and valued.
Images: @poloralphlauren
#Luxurious

posts.caption:
The new Mercedes‑Benz C‑Class Electric marks a decisive step in the electrification strategy of Mercedes‑Benz, bringing one of its most recognisable nameplates into the fully electric era with performance and range figures designed to set new benchmarks in the segment.
Powered by a dual-motor all-wheel-drive system producing up to 482 horsepower, the electric C-Class delivers strong acceleration while maintaining the refined driving character expected from the model line. At its core is a 94 kWh battery paired with an advanced 800-volt electrical architecture, enabling ultra-fast charging and delivering an estimated range of up to 473 miles (around 762 km), placing it among the longest-range electric sedans in its class.
From a design standpoint, the model introduces a more aerodynamic silhouette with sleek lighting signatures and a coupe-inspired profile that reflects the brand’s evolving EV design language. Inside, the cabin focuses on digital integration and luxury comfort, featuring large display interfaces, premium materials, and available technologies such as rear-wheel steering, adaptive air suspension, and an expansive panoramic roof.
With its combination of extended range, high-output performance, and next-generation technology, the electric C-Class positions itself as a cornerstone model in Mercedes-Benz’s transition toward an electrified luxury portfolio.
@mercedesbenzind #MercedesBenz

posts.caption:
#ManifestCover
From engineered gym run-ins to a romance that unfolded at its own pace, Allu Sirish (@allusirish) and Nayanika Reddy’s (@nayanika_reddy) story feels less like a whirlwind and more like a rhythm built over time.
“It wasn’t like we suddenly started going on back-to-back dates. It was more of a slow-burn romance,” says Sirish, recalling how a series of chance meetings — and one very intentional gym switch — finally brought them together.
For Nayanika, the turning point was simpler.
“I realised how easy it felt to be around him. Our conversations were natural, and there was always a sense of comfort. Over time, it became clear that we were building something meaningful,” she shares.
Just days before their wedding, the couple comes together for their first editorial shoot, speaking to Manifest about love that grew organically, families that were always one step ahead, and why the most meaningful relationships don’t need grand gestures to feel certain.
Our latest issue is now live on Magzter. Read the full cover story from the link in bio.

#ManifestDigitalCover | The Shefali Shah Effect
For over three decades, Shefali Shah (@shefalishahofficial) has built a career on instinct, honesty, and an unwavering refusal to fit into boxes. From the unforgettable Pyari in Satya to the formidable DCP Vartika Chaturvedi in Delhi Crime, her performances have never relied on noise — only truth.
“A few years ago, if someone told me a woman in her mid-forties would lead a show, I would have laughed. And if they said it would be me, I would have laughed louder,” she says.
In our latest digital cover, the actor reflects on evolving with time, finding freedom in flawed characters, 25 years of marriage, and why success today means something far quieter than fame.
Our latest issue is now live on Magzter. Read the full story from the link in bio.

However, I am tripping on one rule: Why is it forbidden to button both buttons of a suit jacket?" a reader asked Vanessa Friedman, chief fashion critic for The New York Times. "It gives me better posture and makes me look more together, but am I embarrassing myself if I continue to ignore this?"
When wearing a two-button suit jacket, one is generally advised to leave the bottom button open. At the link in our bio, @vvfriedman explains the reasoning behind the rule. Photo by @apnews

posts.caption:
At Coachella last Friday afternoon, fans rushed to claim a spot in front of one of the festival stages. But the crowd wasn’t camping out for that night’s headliner, Sabrina Carpenter, or for the buzzy girl group Katseye. Attendees were patiently waiting to see Bob Baker Marionette Theater, home to some of Los Angeles’s most popular and beloved puppets.
Started in 1963 by a duo of puppeteers, Bob Baker and Alton Wood, the institution is one of the largest and longest-running puppet theaters in the U.S. But the theater has faced the prospect of closure several times over the past few decades, so performing at Coachella was a major moment for the group.
Mary Fagot, co-executive director of the theater, said that joining the festival lineup felt natural. “We were like, Of course you’re reaching out to us,” Fagot said with a laugh. “Coachella needs us to bring the real and the connected and the joy and the fun and the sense of not taking yourself too seriously.”
Get a closer look at the puppets' Coachella debut and learn more about Bob Baker Marionette Theater at the link in our bio. Visuals by @ye.fan and @dontgabalot
posts.alt_text: Photo by New York Times Fashion & Style on April 18, 2026. Mayor Zohran Mamdani of New York said in an interview with Hell Gate that he would not attend the Met Gala, a star-studded fund-raiser for the Metropolitan Museum of Art’s fashion wing.
Past leaders of the city have made a point of attending the benefit as a show of support for one of New York’s oldest cultural institutions. Michael Bloomberg, Bill de Blasio and Eric Adams have all walked the museum’s famous staircase alongside celebrities, models, athletes, fashion designers and, increasingly, tech billionaires.
Mamdani’s presence at the gala might have seemed out of place at an event that is widely considered to be the playground of the rich and famous. Elected in November on a socialist platform focused on lowering costs for average New Yorkers, Mamdani has made raising taxes on the wealthy a central element of his political brand. Read more about Mamdani’s decision at the link in our bio. Photo by @angelinakatsanis

posts.caption:
They say you can spot a millennial by their self-presentation. Skinny jeans, ankle boots and tucked-in tops are a dead giveaway that someone was born between 1981 and 1996, at least according to a certain online breed of Gen Z. But there's another feature that appears to be a millennial signifier: heavy winged eyeliner.
Popularized by millennial celebrities like Taylor Swift and Ariana Grande, blunt, thick and flicked upward eyeliner rose to prominence in the 2010s, becoming a makeup signature for the generation. While some trends popular in the millennial heyday have seen a recent resurgence — JNCO jeans, “Sex and the City” and photo dumps — the cat eye has given way to the “clean girl aesthetic” favored by Gen Z.
But generational differences over makeup are nothing new, and have long existed across different regions and time periods. Tap the link in our bio to read the resurgence of the eyeliner debate. Photo by Scott a Garfitt/Invision, via @apnews

posts.caption:
The green and cream cap is everywhere. You can buy one at the Masters golf tournament and at 7-Eleven. The athleisure brand Vuori sells one for $48. So did Andy Roddick’s tennis podcast, “Served.” They were $40 but are now sold out.
"In the past couple of years, the two-tone baseball cap has become the red Toyota Camry of the hat market," our fashion reporter Jacob Gallagher wrote. "I saw one. Then I couldn’t stop seeing them."
Look at golf brands (Fore All), basketball lifestyle labels (Monday Hoops Club) and a surf company in landlocked Austin, Texas (Mañana). Team U.S.A. sells one, as did Ray’s Bar in Miami. The Barstool Sports website sells a $38 version that reads “Boy Mom.” Zendaya was photographed in one from Bero, Tom Holland’s nonalcoholic beer brand.
Why has this particular combination become so ubiquitous? At the link in our bio, @jacobwgallagher dives into the trend. Photos by Raymond Hall/GC Images, via Getty Images, Rich Barnes/Getty Images, Raimonda Kulikauskiene/Getty Images, @nytmills

posts.caption:
This week, not only did #Zendaya rewear her 2016 Oscars #VivienneWestwood gown, she also borrowed a Armani dress previously worn by the queen of outfit repetion, #CateBlanchett. It makes you think, should this become more common?
Bound by contracts, loyalty and evolving trends, celebrities have a responsibility to showcase new designers and support the fashion industry. Red carpets function as global advertising platforms providing exposure to talent, but should more celebs rewear their old clothes? By rewearing, new ways of styling and tailoring can transform an existing piece into something nostalgic yet completely new.
Most celebs often wear custom looks that are loaned to them ensuring each appearance feels exclusive and newsworthy. But once photographed and discussed, these garments (which take hundreds of hours to create) are archived. Yet in an increasingly sustainability-conscious landscape, should this change?
What do you think?
Images courtesy of Getty & Backgrid

posts.caption:
They say you can tell when a designer loves the women they’re dressing, and that’s pretty obvious with #SarahBurton’s #Givenchy. Her latest collection moved between tailoring and draping, a reflection of the modern women she is designing for.
One of the most striking looks was the floral dress, an adaptation of a painting by Filipino artist Olan Ventura. Featuring cascading strands of fringe which distorted the floral motifs across the dress so they appeared almost blurred or melting.
The headpieces, by @stephenjonesmillinery , were actually Silk T-shirts twisted and wrapped around the models’ heads, forming sculptural shapes reminiscent of Dutch Golden Age portraiture, specifically Rogier van der Weyden’s Woman with a Winged Bonnet.
What did you think of the collection?

posts.caption:
#Dior has been making some excellent red carpet looks recently and I can’t help but notice @jonathan.anderson’s revival of the Victorian bustle.
The bustle emerged in the late 19th century as a padded or structured undergarment worn beneath skirts to create volume at the back of the body, emphasizing a narrow waist. They relied on layers of fabric, pleating, and internal frameworks. Aptly linking #ChristianDior’s 1947 “New Look,” emphasising a cinched waist and structured hips.
#JonathanAnderson’s reinterpretation focuses on sculptural volume, creating shapes through draped panels, bows, or layered fabrics, creating extremely sculptural and romantic silhouettes. We’ve seen this in gowns, skirts, tailoring, and shorts, featuring pleated or structured volumes attached to the back of garments, expanding the concept of the bustle beyond traditional #HauteCouture.

posts.caption:
Before Al #MarcJacobs was already building theatrical universes for #LouisVuitton, with immense attention to detail and thousands of human hands.
Like Spring 2013, when models came down in pairs on synchronized escalators. Or Fall 2011, the carousel set was a dreamlike pastel fairytale. Fall 2012 perhaps the grandest, when a steam train arrived on the runway and models stepped off, refrecning the house’s heritage in luxury luggage and travel.
That’s the thing people miss when they talk about Al “replacing” creativity. Marc’s sets weren’t impressive just because they were big, they were specifically responding to culture, heritage and mood something only a human brain could understand and create.
What was your favourite set?

First introduced in the 1940s by Marchese #EmilioPucci, the Marmo print became a symbol of the jet-set lifestyle, worn by icons like #JackieKennedy and #MarilynMonroe. Now a defining print of summer, @camillemiceli has reignited it through a modern evolved revival.
#Pucci’s annual cruise shows see a carefully curated group of online “it-girls” who effectively act as ambassadors of a lifestyle, wearing Pucci throughout and documenting every moment. The strategy taps into the “Euro summer” obsession a sense of Mediterranean luxury, with Pucci positioning itself  at the center of it.
The “Pucci-girl summer” embodies carefree glamour and joyful dressing a welcome palette cleanser after years of quiet luxury and old money.
